skill.name: cqrs-implementation
skill.description: Implement Command Query Responsibility Segregation for scalable architectures. Use when separating read and write models, optimizing query performance, or building event-sourced systems.
skill.author: wshobson
skill.summary: Guide CQRS implementation with command handlers, query handlers, read models, and consistency patterns.

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command, network, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows they are documentation false positives. The skill contains a Markdown guide with Python CQRS examples, SQL snippets, and reference links, with no executable installer, hidden command execution, prompt injection, or data exfiltration evidence.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick hits are Markdown code fence delimiters around diagrams and Python examples. No evidence found of executable shell commands or command injection behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched tokens are Markdown code block delimiters. The surrounding content is a diagram or Python template text, not executable shell syntax.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Database Calls and Reference Links
low_findings.description: The fetch hit is a database query example, not an HTTP Fetch API call. The hardcoded URLs are resource links to CQRS documentation and do not send data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 283 is inside a Python database access example. Lines 551 and 552 are Markdown references, with no code that performs outbound requests.

low_findings.title: Keyword Matches Are Benign CQRS Example Terms
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are keyword matches in architecture prose, sort order strings, UUID creation, SQL fields, and identifiers. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, host inspection, or environment probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal CQRS terms and sample SQL at these lines. There is no code path that performs cryptography, system reconnaissance, or credential collection.

content.value_statement: Complex applications often mix reads and writes, which limits scaling and clarity. This skill guides CQRS separation with Python templates, handlers, projections, and consistency patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains when CQRS fits read and write separation needs.
Provides Python templates for command buses, query buses, and handlers.
Shows FastAPI endpoints separated by command and query responsibilities.
Demonstrates read model synchronization from events and checkpoints.
Covers read-your-writes behavior for eventual consistency.
limitations: Does not include a runnable package or automated project generator.
Uses illustrative Python snippets that need adaptation for production systems.
Does not provide database migrations, deployment scripts, or monitoring setup.
Assumes the user understands asynchronous Python and basic architecture patterns.

use_cases.title: Separate Read and Write Workloads
use_cases.description: Design command and query paths for an application that needs independent scaling and clearer ownership.

use_cases.title: Add Event-Sourced Projections
use_cases.description: Plan read model projection logic that updates query views from stored domain events.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Monolithic Service
use_cases.description: Identify command handlers, query handlers, and API boundaries before splitting application behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Assess CQRS Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my application context and tell me whether CQRS is appropriate. Focus on read volume, write complexity, consistency needs, and added operational cost.

prompt_templates.title: Design Command and Query Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CQRS design for this domain. Define commands, command handlers, queries, query handlers, read models, and validation boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Projection Synchronization
prompt_templates.prompt: Design read model synchronization for these domain events. Include projection checkpoints, replay behavior, retry handling, and stale data behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Review CQRS Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this CQRS implementation for coupling, consistency gaps, command validation, projection reliability, query performance, and production readiness.

output_examples.input: A retail API needs faster customer order queries without slowing order creation.
output_examples.output: A CQRS design separates order creation commands from customer order query views, with projections updating denormalized read tables from order events.

output_examples.input: A team wants read-your-writes behavior after a command is accepted.
output_examples.output: The response plan includes expected event versions, projection checkpoints, bounded waiting, timeout handling, and a stale data warning when needed.

output_examples.input: An existing service has handlers that both mutate state and build reports.
output_examples.output: The refactor plan identifies command handlers for state changes and query handlers backed by optimized reporting read models.

best_practices: Start with clear consistency requirements before introducing CQRS complexity.
Keep command validation close to the write model and business rules.
Design read models around real query patterns, not write model structure.
anti_patterns: Applying CQRS to simple CRUD features with no scaling or model separation need.
Letting commands depend on query models for core business decisions.
Ignoring projection lag, replay safety, and failure recovery.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete CQRS application?
faq.answer: No. It provides templates and architecture guidance that users adapt to their own application.

faq.question: Is CQRS the same as event sourcing?
faq.answer: No. CQRS separates reads from writes, while event sourcing stores state changes as events.

faq.question: Can I use this with FastAPI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes FastAPI-style examples for command and query endpoints.

faq.question: Does this require separate databases?
faq.answer: No. Separate databases are optional, but read and write models should have clear boundaries.

faq.question: How should I handle eventual consistency?
faq.answer: Define acceptable lag, track projection checkpoints, and return stale data warnings when strict freshness is unavailable.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation false positives and no executable malicious behavior.
